titleStmt.title: 59. Telegram From the Special Representative in Vietnam (Collins) to the ) to the Department of State

div.head: 59. Telegram From the Special Representative in Vietnam (Collins) to the Department of State Source: Department of State, Central Files, 751G.00/3–1055. Secret. Repeated to Paris, Phnom Penh, and Vientiane.
div.p: 3797. 1. I called on Ely prior his departure for Paris 10th. He said he was going to make contact with new government and review budget and perhaps political questions. I asked if he had assurance of funds sufficient, with U.S. aid, to maintain FEC troop level he desires. He replied he had no intention of going below 75,000. At present he is evacuating 10 to 14 thousand troops per month, a figure he thinks too high and might give idea that FEC is being withdrawn entirely. He hopes level off shortly to a lower rate which could be steadily continued in event Vietnam Government should decide to request complete evacuation. He feels 75,000 is minimum force with which he can face troubles in South Vietnam if they should occur.

2. Ely visited Cambodia and Laos 8th. He feels situation Cambodia is not serious. Agreement appears exist between old and new kingsOn March 3, Sihanouk relinquished his crown in favor of his father who became King Norodom Suramarit. regarding holding elections under conditions favorable to government. Sihanouk thinks he can wage successful election campaign against leftist politicians. Ely noted Sihanouk is still in public eye and remains Commander-in-Chief of ARK. Although new king has
been making some show of independence against Sihanouk, Ely feels the father has no intention of contesting son’s position.

3. Ely had lengthy conversation in Vientiane with Prime Minister Kathay regarding two northern provinces. Ely said danger that Pathet Lao might be taken into coalition government now seems averted. He had also feared Laos might re-open hostilities under unfavorable conditions in an attempt to establish authority in northern provinces. This danger also seems averted but still occasions him some concern. I asked Ely how he proposed Laos Government get provinces under control. He replied this should be done through diplomatic channels. He said so far only French have protested Pathet Lao activities. He would propose at some stage that Laos appeal to Geneva powers if ICC were unable obtain satisfaction from Laos Government. I said I had impression ICC favored Communist position. Ely replied that such impression from outside was understandable. However, Indian position has changed appreciably in favor of non-Communists, and Canadians, long intent on strict neutrality, now appear leaning to this side. This evolution should permit satisfactory solution of Laos problem. Otherwise the Geneva powers might be asked to intervene. I asked what the powers could do. Ely replied that ultimate appeal might be made to SEATO. However, Lao Government must meanwhile be careful avoid appearance of aggression. I asked how under Geneva Accord Lao Government could be labeled aggressor if it took defensive action in its own territory. Ely said Laotians might in course of military action cross frontiers and lay selves open to charge of technical aggression. Ely commented that recent influx of visitors following Bangkok Conference, all of whom proferred advice to governments of the Associated States, may have had somewhat disturbing effect. He summarized his position on Laos as calling for appeal first to ICC then to Geneva powers and only as last resort to SEATO.

4. With reference Vietnam, Ely said political situation was extremely confused owing to action of sects and difference of views within the government itself. I asked him how he evaluated recent sect manifesto.On March 1, the Cao Dai Pope, Pham Cong Tac, held a press conference and announced that the Cao Dai, Binh Xuyen, and the Hoa Hao had formed a “United Front of Nationalist Forces” and set forth their manifesto. An analysis of the front and the manifesto is in telegram 3754 from Saigon, March 8. (Department of State, Central Files, 751G.00/3–855) Ely replied he saw it as warning to government. He believes sects do not intend to take military action and that manifesto may have been made for blackmail purposes. Ely said that several emissaries from Bao Dai to the sects had been and perhaps still were on the local scene. However he did not know who they were or exactly what they were doing. He said that while this may not be the
most difficult political period we have gone through, it is that in which most maneuvering has taken place. I commented that some of our people take the sect manifesto seriously, although they do not foresee military action, while others see it rather as a sign of weakness on the part of the sects. Ely said that his advisors were equally divided in opinion. I said to Ely the sects’ reaction appeared to me to be inevitable and that we should not be too greatly surprised or disturbed as long as we watched situation carefully and advised Vietnamese Government on countermeasures. I said I had asked my staff to consider if manifesto could be used by government to own advantage against sects through capitalizing on idea of unity and of suppression of private armies and abandonment of feudal system expressed in sects’ manifesto.

5. Ely agreed this approach would be useful but added that there was another aspect which concerned him. General Phuong(Cao Dai) for example has 15,000 “suppletive” troops no longer paid for by French but must be cared for by Cao Dai. I said that we hoped for peaceful solution to problem and suggested that troops discharged from sect armies might be placed on the land as part of the agrarian reform. To enable sect leaders bridge awkward hiatus, we might attempt develop plan for severance pay for such sect forces to give temporary relief on condition that arms be turned in and that no action be taken against government. American aid might be given to settle discharged sect troops on land along lines of refugee and agrarian program. If French were able to provide funds for severance pay, we might help in resettlement. Ely and I agreed set up special group, operating within TRIM (special section in MAAG under Colonel Lansdale handling pacification program) to consider this problem.For Lansdale’s account of his work on this special group and Collins’ adverse reaction to its recommendations, see In the Midst of Wars, pp. 248–252.

6. I remarked that Binh Xuyen appeared most dangerous group at moment. I told Ely that when President’s letter was delivered to Bao Dai, Paris Embassy officer had explored with the Chief of State question of withdrawing Binh Xuyen control of police. Bao Dai’s action had been entirely negative. Ely said it was impossible Bao Dai not support Binh Xuyen since Bay Vien is Bao Dai’s firmest supporter and still important source of Imperial revenue. Ely remarked that although Bay Vien is now heading sect coalition, he might easily be won over by the government. I asked what Diem could offer him. Ely said Bay Vien wishes to be reassured rather than to obtain material benefits. I said Diem cannot reassure Bay Vien, who is acting against government in many fields. Ely said that worst tactic for Diem to adopt would be to turn his back on Bay Vien. As long as contact is maintained, possibility still exists to work out some solution.
I said I would reflect on this matter but was at a loss to see what Diem could offer.

7. Ely said that within the government there appeared to be many men working against Diem. Although they may be performing their daily functions, some of these men seem to be seeking alliance with the sects while others are turning to Bao Dai. The only solution Ely had for this problem was that Diem should make the government work as a government and not rely, as he does, on his family. He said he would again convey this idea to Diem today.

8. I told Ely that I was still not clear as to the status of withdrawal of U.S.-financed Charbonnages equipment from the North. He said that he had given General Cogny orders to evacuate the material and would see him on this subject before Ely’s departure.

9. I told Ely that I had learned through his subordinates that he felt our political survey and re-evaluation of the Diem government could be postponed. Ely said there is no urgency about a meeting in Washington on this problem. France and U.S. are in agreement to support Diem. He believed a Washington meeting might be held to discuss larger questions at about the time the Haiphong enclave is evacuated. Ely said that he had expressed this opinion to the French Government. I will take no further action but will pouch copy of our draft of survey seen by Secretary in Saigon to which Ely had given general but not formal agreement.
10. In reply to my question Ely said that during his absence his deputy for military affairs would be General Jacquot, but that no one would substitute for him in the civil sphere.
11. On way out I asked de Rougemont about rumors of Ely’s relief. He said Ely undoubtedly will be relieved but probably not till evacuation of Haiphong.
